title: The Smale conjecture for RP^3 and minimal surfaces
speaker: Daniel Ketover
abstract: In the early 80s Hatcher proved the Smale Conjecture, asserting that the diffeomorphism group of the three-sphere retracts onto its isometry group.  The corresponding problem for RP^3 was open nearly 40 years, and resolved only in 2019 by a detailed study of Ricci flow with surgeries.  I will explain a new proof using minimal surfaces.  This is joint work with Yevgeny Liokumovich.

proof_stages.label: Smale-conjecture paradigm and topological prerequisites
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the problem of when the inclusion of the isometry group into the diffeomorphism group is a homotopy equivalence, reviews the classical low-dimensional cases and the hoped-for geometric-flow approach, and records Hatcher/Ivanov contractibility results for handlebodies and related Haken manifolds that will later give contractible fibers.

proof_stages.label: Known constant-curvature landscape and main theorem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker specializes to closed three-manifolds of constant sectional curvature, reviews known hyperbolic and spherical-space-form results, and states the Liokumovich–Marques theorem proving the Smale conjecture for RP^3 and lens spaces by minimal-surface methods, excluding S^3.

proof_stages.label: Clifford tori as the canonical minimal Heegaard tori in RP^3
proof_stages.summary: Using RP^3=S^3/(±1), Brendle's classification of embedded minimal tori in S^3, and the topology of the complement, the speaker identifies every embedded minimal torus in round RP^3 as a Clifford torus of area pi^2 and index one, splitting RP^3 into two genus-one handlebodies, with Clifford-torus space RP^2 x RP^2.

proof_stages.label: Reformulation by Heegaard-torus embeddings
proof_stages.summary: Fixing a reference Clifford torus, the projection Diff(RP^3) to the space of unknotted Heegaard torus embeddings is shown to be a fibration with contractible fiber, so the Smale conjecture becomes the assertion that the full embedding space retracts onto the Clifford-torus subspace, equivalently that the relative homotopy groups vanish.

proof_stages.label: Simon–Smith min-max and the RP^3 one-parameter width
proof_stages.summary: The analytic input is the Simon–Smith min-max theorem for multi-parameter families of genus-g surfaces, strengthened by multiplicity-one results, and its application to the standard genus-one sweepout of RP^3, whose min-max limit must be a Clifford torus and hence has width pi^2.

proof_stages.label: Minimal counterexample and sweepout extension
proof_stages.summary: Assuming a least nontrivial relative homotopy class, the speaker represents it by a disk of Heegaard tori with Clifford boundary and extends it to a cylinder by adding a sweepout parameter, arranging that each vertical line is a nontrivial Heegaard sweepout and boundary lines are optimal CMC foliations.

proof_stages.label: High-parameter width forced to equal pi^2
proof_stages.summary: For the homotopy class of the extended family, the boundary maximum is pi^2, and if the width were larger then min-max would produce only a Clifford torus of area pi^2, a contradiction; therefore the high-parameter width is exactly pi^2.

proof_stages.label: Near-Clifford relative cycle and topological closure
proof_stages.summary: Pull-tight sweepouts with maximal area tending to pi^2 yield, on each vertical line, slices weakly close to Clifford tori and hence a relative cycle homologous to the original disk; Hatcher-type retractions push these near-Clifford tori into tubular neighborhoods and then to Clifford tori, and the Hurewicz theorem upgrades the resulting null homology to the required null homotopy.

examples.name: Circle case of the Smale question
examples.note: The speaker uses Diff(S^1), viewed via increasing functions on an interval fixing a point, to make the homotopy retraction onto O(2) concrete in the simplest case.
